one_line_takeaway: e-SeaFL delivers single-round, dropout-robust secure aggregation for federated learning that is orders of magnitude cheaper for users/server on large gradient vectors and adds malicious-server verifiability via homomorphic commitments, at the cost of introducing assisting nodes (>=1 honest) and an expensive user-side integrity proof.
key_finding: For high-dimensional (16,000-weight) gradient vectors, e-SeaFL's per-user aggregation-phase computation is roughly 5 orders of magnitude faster than MicroSecAgg and 33-119x faster than Flamingo regular users (2.1 ms semi-honest / 4.8 ms malicious at 1,000 users, and constant in the number of users). Server aggregation compute is 5-6 orders of magnitude faster than MicroSecAgg and 264-499x (semi-honest) / 205-386x (malicious) faster than Flamingo. Overall aggregation-phase overhead is up to 55,241x/65,264x faster than MicroSecAgg1/2 and up to 41x faster than Flamingo (malicious). Crucially it needs only k=2-3 assisting nodes versus Flamingo's >=64 decryptors, and adds no accuracy loss versus non-private FedAvg on EMNIST and CIFAR-100.
core_contribution: The paper proposes e-SeaFL, a verifiable secure-aggregation protocol for privacy-preserving federated learning that needs only ONE communication round in the aggregation phase (plus a one-time setup reusable across many training iterations). Its two distinctive contributions are: (1) a small set of k 'assisting nodes' (as few as k=2) that each share a secret seed with a user and supply the aggregation server with pre-aggregated masking terms, so masks cancel out in one round while privacy holds as long as just ONE assisting node is honest; and (2) a 'proof of honest aggregation' built on an authenticated Pedersen vector commitment (APVC), letting users verify the final aggregated model was computed correctly by a malicious server. The masking design (pairwise key-agreement seeds feeding a PRF so masks sum to zero) and the reliance on assisting nodes are the authors' own construction; Flamingo's decryptors and MicroSecAgg's exponent-aggregation are cited prior work the paper compares against, not e-SeaFL's own mechanism.
threat_model: Two threat models are stated by the authors. Privacy: an adversary controls the aggregation server plus a bounded fraction (1-alpha) of users and up to k-1 of the k assisting nodes, and tries to learn any individual user's local gradient w_Pi; it may also drop or disconnect a delta fraction of users. Both semi-honest (honest-but-curious, colluding-view) and malicious (protocol-deviating) adversaries are handled, with only one honest assisting node required for privacy. Integrity: a malicious BUT non-colluding aggregation server that tries to dishonestly aggregate updates; users detect this via the homomorphic commitment check g^(wt*rho)=xt. Explicitly OUT of scope: malicious clients poisoning/backdooring the model (would need access to private training data), and post-deployment inference attacks such as membership inference (deferred to differential privacy as future work).
limitations: 1) e-SeaFL introduces new trusted-ish infrastructure (assisting nodes); privacy rests on at least one of them being honest, a different assumption than pure user-honesty protocols. 2) The malicious-server integrity proof is expensive on the USER side: computing the authenticated commitment takes ~2.6 minutes due to unoptimized EC scalar multiplication (authors note Pippenger / low-level implementations could cut this). 3) No protection against malicious clients submitting poisoned/backdoored weights (acknowledged out of scope). 4) Inference attacks (e.g., membership inference) at deployment are not addressed; DP integration is left as future work. 5) Integrity requires the malicious server to be non-colluding with users. 6) Fairness caveat in the evaluation: MicroSecAgg only reported single-gradient numbers, so its 16,000-weight figures were PROJECTED by the authors rather than measured; server bandwidth also grows linearly due to a one-time ECDSA key broadcast.

datasets_used: Extended MNIST (EMNIST) [40]: 340,000 training / 40,000 test samples, 28x28 images, 10 classes; ~220 images per user; MLP classifier; yields an 8,000 x 32-bit weight vector
CIFAR-100 [41]: 50,000 training / 10,000 test samples, 32x32 images, 100 classes; ~100 images per user; CNN with normalization layers; yields a 500,000 x 32-bit weight vector
Synthetic benchmark configuration: 200-1000 users, k=3 assisting nodes, 16,000-weight gradient vectors (also single-gradient for fair comparison)
quantitative_results: Aggregation phase, per-user compute at 1,000 users: 2.1 ms (semi-honest) / 4.8 ms (malicious), constant in number of users
Aggregation compute vs MicroSecAgg (16,000 gradients): ~5 orders of magnitude faster for users; ~5-6 orders of magnitude faster for the server
Aggregation compute vs Flamingo: user 33-119x faster (regular), 118-396x faster (decryptor); server 264-499x (SH) / 205-386x (MS) faster
Overall aggregation overhead: up to 55,241x (vs MicroSecAgg1) and 65,264x (vs MicroSecAgg2) faster; up to 41x faster than Flamingo (malicious)
Setup per-user compute: 0.18 ms (SH) / 0.25 ms (MS) vs 2,509 ms (MicroSecAgg1) and 33 ms (MicroSecAgg2) at 1,000 users; 142x/283x faster than Flamingo64/128 regular users
Setup per-user outbound bandwidth: 99 B (SH) / 231 B (MS) vs ~905 KB (MicroSecAgg1), ~183 KB (MicroSecAgg2), 2,013 B (Flamingo64), 3,612 B (Flamingo128)
Overall setup compute: ~150 ms (e-SeaFL) vs 2,885 ms (MicroSecAgg1), 210 ms (MicroSecAgg2), 36,720 ms (Flamingo64), 302,490 ms (Flamingo128)
Assisting-node aggregation compute: 136-681 ms (SH) / 128-722 ms (MS) for 200-1,000 users; memory ~1.5 GB at 200 users with 16,000 weights
Integrity proof: user-side APVC commitment ~2.6 min (unoptimized EC scalar mult); server aggregation over commitments 4.02-20.72 ms; server integrity bandwidth 12,800-64,000 B for 200-1,000 users
Minimum assisting nodes k=2 (evaluated k=3) versus Flamingo's requirement of >=64 active decryptors per iteration
Model accuracy under e-SeaFL matches non-private FedAvg on EMNIST and CIFAR-100 over up to 300 training rounds (no accuracy degradation)
baselines_compared: Bell et al. BBG+20 [9] (analytical comparison of setup/aggregation costs)
MicroSecAgg [10] - two instantiations: MicroSecAgg1 (MicroSecAgg_DL, star topology) and MicroSecAgg2 (MicroSecAgg_gDL, group size 50), semi-honest
Flamingo [11] - Flamingo64 and Flamingo128 (64/128 decryptors), malicious, with regular-user (R) and with-decryptor (w/D) variants
FedAvg [1] - non-private plaintext FL, used as the model-accuracy baseline
